planting.note: Plant tubers horizontally in a container of heavy clay soil and submerge in a pond or water garden after the water reaches 65°F; the curved tip of the tuber should point upward.

directions: Plant tubers in heavy clay-loam soil (not potting mix, which will float) in containers placed in 12–24 inches of water in full sun; the massive circular leaves and huge pale-yellow flowers up to 10 inches across are among the most spectacular sights in water gardening. All parts of this native aquatic plant are edible — the starchy rhizomes are eaten like potatoes, the seeds like nuts, and the young leaves and flower petals as vegetables. The large, sculptural seed heads remain on the plant through winter and are popular in dried floral arrangements. In a natural pond, the rhizomes can spread aggressively; use containers to control spread in small water features.

planting.note: Plant small tubers 2–3 inches deep in spring after last frost; provide a trellis or fence for the twining stems. Tubers from the previous year planted in spring produce the largest harvest in fall.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun near a support for the twining stems; this native leguminous vine was a staple food for many Indigenous nations and early European settlers and was considered for commercial cultivation before potatoes became dominant. Harvest the small, walnut-shaped tubers strung along underground rhizomes in fall after frost kills the vines; they taste like a richer, nuttier potato and can be boiled, baked, or roasted. The fragrant reddish-purple flowers in midsummer fix atmospheric nitrogen. Tubers are small in the first year but grow larger each subsequent year — the plant becomes more productive the longer it is left undisturbed.
sources: Edible Wild Plants: A North American Field Guide

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface 2–4 weeks before last frost; press seed lightly into soil as light aids germination. Division of established clumps is faster than seeding.

directions: Sow seed on the soil surface (light required for germination) in moist, average soil in partial shade to full sun; this native ageratum look-alike fills the critical late-season window with fluffy blue-lavender flower clusters that attract migrating monarchs and late-season bees from August through first frost. Plant in large drifts for the greatest visual impact and pollinator value. The plant spreads aggressively by rhizome in moist, fertile soil — divide every 2–3 years in spring to control spread and maintain vigor. Excellent companion for goldenrods and fall asters in a native pollinator garden.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seeds for 60–90 days then sow in spring, or direct sow in fall for natural stratification; first bloom occurs in the second or third year.

directions: Sow cold-stratified seed or plant transplants in deep, well-drained, preferably rocky or clay loam soil in full sun; this long-lived prairie plant grows a massive, 6-foot-deep taproot that anchors it against drought and enables it to survive prairie fire. The basal rosette of enormous, rough-textured heart-shaped leaves — up to 18 inches across — is distinctive all season, while the wiry branched stems carry yellow composite flowers to 8 feet in mid- to late summer. It is extremely slow to establish, requiring 3–4 years to first bloom, but once rooted it is virtually indestructible and may live for decades. Do not transplant established plants as the taproot cannot be severed without killing the plant.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in highly acidic, humus-rich soil; requires consistent moisture and does not tolerate heat or dry conditions — best suited to northern gardens and high elevations.

directions: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), consistently moist, conifer-needle-rich soil in full to partial shade; this diminutive groundcover grows naturally in boreal and mixed forests under spruce and fir where acid humus accumulates thickly. The four-petaled white flowers with green centers (like tiny dogwood blossoms) appear in spring, followed by clusters of bright red berries in summer that are edible, mildly sweet, and popular with thrushes, waxwings, and grouse. Amend soil heavily with aged pine bark, peat moss, or decomposed conifer needles to achieve the required acidity before planting. Heat and alkaline soil are the two primary causes of failure; this is not a plant for the southern United States or urban heat islands.
